perturbation.original_text: 3.1 Compliance with Local Laws and Regulations The Manufacturer covenants that it is and will remain for the term of this agreement in compliance with any and all Local Laws and Regulations. This includes without limitation laws relating to business practice, workplace relations, safety and taxation.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 Compliance with Local Laws and Regulations The Manufacturer covenants that it will endeavor to comply with Local Laws and Regulations to the best of its understanding. This may include laws relating to business practice, workplace relations, safety and taxation.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'is and will remain in compliance' to 'will endeavor to comply...to the best of its understanding' introduces ambiguity. It weakens the obligation to a subjective standard, making enforcement difficult. Using "may include" instead of "includes without limitation" limits the examples provided.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Failure to comply with workplace safety regulations
perturbation.law_explanation: OSHA 29 CFR § 1910.132 requires employers to provide and ensure the use of personal protective equipment (PPE) where necessary. The modified clause allows the manufacturer to subjectively decide on compliance ('to the best of its understanding') which can lead to inadequate PPE and therefore a breach of federal law. It makes compliance discretionary rather than mandatory, violating OSHA regulations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 1910.132 - General requirements. | Occupational Safety and Health Administration Skip to main content Home By Standard Number 1910.132 - General requirements. Part Number: 1910 Part Number Title: Occupational Safety and Health Standards Subpart: 1910 Subpart I Subpart Title: Personal Protective Equipment Standard Number: 1910.132 Title: General requirements. GPO Source: e-CFR 1910.132(a) Application. Protective equipment, including personal protective equipment for eyes, face, head, and extremities, protective clothing, respiratory devices, and protective shields and barriers, shall be provided, used, and maintained in a sanitary and reliable condition wherever it is necessary by reason of hazards of processes or environment, chemical hazards, radiological hazards, or mechanical irritants encountered in a manner capable of causing injury or impairment in the function of any part of the body through absorption, inhalation or physical contact. 1910.132(b) Employee-owned equipment. Where employees provide their own protective equipment, the employer shall be responsible to assure its adequacy, including proper maintenance, and sanitation of such equipment. 1910.132(c) Design. All personal protective equipment shall be of safe design and construction for the work to be performed. 1910.132(d) Hazard assessment and equipment selection. 1910.132(d)(1) The employer shall assess the workplace to determine if hazards are present, or are likely to be present, which necessitate the use of personal protective equipment (PPE). If such hazards are present, or likely to be present, the employer shall: 1910.132(d)(1)(i) Select, and have each affected employee use, the types of PPE that will protect the affected employee from the hazards identified in the hazard assessment; 1910.132(d)(1)(ii) Communicate selection decisions to each affected employee; and, 1910.132(d)(1)(iii) Select PPE that properly fits each affected employee. Note: Non-mandatory appendix B contains an example of procedures

CFR prev | next § 1910.132 General requirements. (a) Application. Protective equipment, including personal protective equipment for eyes, face, head, and extremities, protective clothing , respiratory devices, and protective shields and barriers, shall be provided, used, and maintained in a sanitary and reliable condition wherever it is necessary by reason of hazards of processes or environment, chemical hazards, radiological hazards, or mechanical irritants encountered in a manner capable of causing injury or impairment in the function of any part of the body through absorption, inhalation or physical contact. (b) Employee-owned equipment. Where employees provide their own protective equipment, the employer shall be responsible to assure its adequacy, including proper maintenance, and sanitation of such equipment. (c) Design. All personal protective equipment shall be of safe design and construction for the work to be performed. (d) Hazard assessment and equipment selection. (1) The employer shall assess the workplace to determine if hazards are present, or are likely to be present, which necessitate the use of personal protective equipment (PPE). If such hazards are present, or likely to be present, the employer shall: (i) Select, and have each affected employee use , the types of PPE that will protect the affected employee from the hazards identified in the hazard assessment; (ii) Communicate selection decisions to each affected employee ; and, (iii) Select PPE that properly fits each affected employee . Note: Non-mandatory appendix B contains an example of procedures that would comply with the requirement for a hazard assessment. (2) The employer shall verify that the required workplace hazard assessment has been performed through a written certification th
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates compliance with local laws, while the changed text only requires the manufacturer to 'endeavor to comply...to the best of its understanding'. This introduces a subjective standard that weakens the obligation. OSHA 29 CFR § 1910.132(a), as stated in both scraped snippets, requires that protective equipment 'shall be provided, used, and maintained'. The changed text, by allowing the manufacturer to subjectively determine compliance, contradicts the mandatory nature of the PPE requirement under OSHA. This creates an ambiguity that can result in a clear non-compliance with the law.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1 Compliance with Local Laws and Regulations The Customer covenants that it is and will remain for the term of this agreement in compliance with any and all Local Laws and Regulations. This includes without limitation laws relating to business practice, workplace relations, safety and taxation.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1 Compliance with Local Laws and Regulations The Customer covenants that it will attempt to comply with Local Laws and Regulations where feasible. This includes laws relating to business practice and taxation.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'is and will remain in compliance' with 'will attempt to comply where feasible' weakens the legal obligation by making compliance conditional. The phrase 'where feasible' is subjective and open to interpretation. By removing 'workplace relations, safety,' the obligation is further weakened.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Failure to comply with minimum wage laws
perturbation.law_explanation: 29 U.S. Code § 206 mandates employers to pay employees a minimum wage. The modification allows the customer to only 'attempt to comply where feasible,' which could lead to employees not being paid the minimum wage, violating the Fair Labor Standards Act. The vague nature of "where feasible" does not meet the obligation of the law.
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces a strict compliance requirement with a conditional one ('attempt to comply where feasible'), directly undermining the original obligation to adhere to all local laws and regulations. This is particularly concerning in the context of labor laws, as suggested by the Law Explanation regarding minimum wage, where feasibility cannot be a valid excuse for non-compliance. The original text required full compliance, while the changed text weakens this requirement, creating a potential contradiction with laws mandating specific actions without exceptions based on feasibility.

perturbation.original_text: 5.2 Guarantee of packaging quality The Manufacturer further guarantees that the Products, when shipped, are packaged in such a way as to be protected from any foreseeable damage during shipment.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.2 Guarantee of packaging quality The Manufacturer aims to package the Products adequately for shipment, taking into account potential damage.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'guarantees that the Products...are packaged in such a way as to be protected from any foreseeable damage' to 'aims to package the Products adequately for shipment, taking into account potential damage' significantly weakens the obligation. 'Aims' indicates an intention rather than a binding guarantee. 'Adequately' is vague, and there is no commitment to prevent foreseeable damage.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Liability for damaged goods in transit under the UCC
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-319 (although primarily defining shipping terms) implies certain obligations for ensuring goods are properly prepared for shipment. By removing the firm guarantee of protection from foreseeable damage and replacing it with a weaker 'aims to package adequately', the manufacturer reduces its responsibility for goods damaged during shipment. This contradicts the expected standard of care and obligations outlined by commercial law pertaining to product guarantees.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: rship that put all the component commercial laws together in a comprehensive Uniform Commercial Code that was offered to the states for their consideration in 1951. Pennsylvania became the first state to adopt the UCC in 1953, and every other state followed suit over the next twenty years. News ULC Statement on Ownership of Investment Property under Uniform Commercial Code Article 8 ULC Statement on Central Bank Digital Currencies and the Uniform Commercial Code Cooperation with ALI The UCC is a joint project of the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute. Recognizing that drafting a combined commercial code was a massive undertaking, the ULC invited ALI to participate in the codification project, and the ALI board accepted the invitation in 1942. Over the next ten years the two organizations collaborated at drafting meetings funded in large part by a generous grant from the Maurice and Laura Falk Foundation, with additional funding contributed from law firms, banks, and businesses that recognized the need for uniform commercial laws. The UCC Today The UCC is maintained under the guidance of the Permanent Editorial Board for the Uniform Commercial Code (PEB) , comprised of members appointed by the ULC and the ALI. The PEB, established in 1961, monitors developments in commercial law, recommends UCC amendments and revisions when necessary, and publishes official commentary to help courts interpret specific UCC provisions. An endowment established with the original Falk Foundation grant funding and replenished with UCC publishing royalties is available to fund UCC drafting projects. Articles Article 1, General Provisions Uniform Commercial Code Article 1 contains definitions and general provisions applicable as default rules to transactions covered under other articles of the UCC. Article 1 was last revised in 2001, with a few minor amendments since then to harmonize with recent revisions of other UCC articles. View Article 1, General Provisions Article 2, Sales Uniform Commercial Code Article 2 governs the sale of goods. It was part of the original Uniform Commercial Code approved in 1951. Article 2 represented a revision and modernization of the Uniform Sales Act, which was originally approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1906. The Uniform Law Commission and American Law Institute approved a revised Article 2 in 2003 that was not adopted in any state, and was subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus the 1951 version of Article 2 is the most recent official version. View Article 2, Sales Article 2A, Leases Uniform Commercial Code Article 2A governs leases of personal property. It was first added to the Uniform Commercial Code in 1987 and amended in 1990. A revision was approved by the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute in 2003, but was not adopted in any jurisdiction and subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus, the 1987 version of Article 2A, as amended in 1990, remains the official text. View Article 2A, Leases Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Uniform Commercial Code Article 3 governs negotiable instruments: drafts (including checks) and notes representing a promise to pay a sum of money, and that have independent value because they are negotiable. An instrument is negotiable if it can be transferred to another person and remain enforceable against the person who originally made the promise to pay. The substance of Article 3 has its roots in the Negotiable Instrument Law first approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1896. That early uniform law was revised and incorporated into the original version of the UCC in 1951, and a further revision was approved in 1990. Finally, a set of amendments to UCC Articles 3 and 4 was approved in 2002. View Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Article 4, Bank Deposits and Collections Uniform Commercial Code Article 4 governs bank deposits and collecti
perturbation.justification: The original text guarantees protection from foreseeable damage during shipment, creating a clear obligation. The changed text weakens this to 'aims to package adequately,' which only expresses an intention and uses the vague term 'adequately'. The Perturbation Explanation notes this weakens the obligation, and the Law Explanation suggests it contradicts the expected standard of care for product guarantees. While the provided scraped law snippet does not directly address this, the UCC aims to create uniform commercial laws, implying a standard of care for ensuring goods are properly prepared for shipment, per the LLM Law Explanation. The change introduces ambiguity where there was certainty, creating a contradiction.
